It will do so by providing guidance to coders on how to assign Type 1, 2, 3 or 4 status to qualitative text including, but not limited to, peer reviewed journal articles, course syllabi, policy statements, policy analyses, media communications and policy recommendations. This classification effort complements, rather than replaces, careful qualitative application of the framework required for uncovering important nuances through deliberative analysis and reflection of highly complex text. ***What the quantitative coding effort does well is to generate useful knowledge about whether the overall patterns identified by an one study or set of studies compare to other cases outside of the study. Coding also permits assessments as to whether there are differences in the dominance of one or more problem types across time and also space – be they countries, organizations, or sectors.*** Hence, not only can a well-developed code book generate descriptions about a range of policy and governance outcomes, it will also allow for broadening epistemological and methodological techniques that might explain variation – such as the correlation between the kind of disciplinary, sub-disciplinary and interdisciplinary training experts received that shape their bias in emphasizing one Type over the other. This in turn, might help generate knowledge about, and reverse when desired, “unconscious bias” about the way policy scholars and practitioners conceive of, and measure, “on the ground” problems. In other words, this framework can help advance “fit for purpose” policy analysis. To be clear: unlike Cashore’s broader argument about the drift away from Type 4 problems that he has, and is, applying to understand their role in shaping sustainability policy and governance over the last 35 years, the code book is designed to describe a range of outcomes, to which Cashore and his collaborators, and anyone else for that matter, can better assess, test, and develop their own arguments and theories. In short, the code book, paves the way for, but does not take a position on, which of the four problem types is preferred nor the causes of changes in emphasis of the Types. Importantly, although the 4PT distinctions originally came out of Cashore’s work on global, transnational and local approaches to sustainability policy and governance, they can be applied to disentangle how any kind of problem facing society and policy making are analysed, and treated, by scholars and experts. This means that ***while Cashore applies and draws examples from sustainability policy scholarship and practice, the framework is equally useful for anyone working on problem focused policy analysis including, but not limited to those scholars working in schools of public health, education, social work, forestry, fisheries, natural resources, agriculture, and even mining and energy for that matter.*** Having said this, the guidance and examples below for coders to be able to render concrete important but sometimes abstract distinctions, draw on examples from five years of efforts to code sustainability policy and governance analyses. This code book proceeds in the following steps. First, we discuss Cashore’s “utility” and “problem solving” labels as the two key distinctions that position the 4PT framework. Second, we review Cashore’s four key schools of sustainability thought and practice – including notable strands within them – to show how each of these 4PTs are subtly nurtured by sophisticated scholarly communities. Third, we review the challenges facing coding of different kinds of qualitative documents. Fourth, we review different sorting challenges based on the kinds of document being including abstracts of course syllabi, journal articles. Fifth we offer solutions to each including reviewing of our protocol for sorting scholarly articles through careful qualitative analysis that has been developed and revised over the last five years. We then review our approach to coding course syllabi and the development of key words to facilitate swift coding of a range of documents. We then discuss our systematic efforts to advance AI assisted coding with the goal of producing systematic text analysis that would be consistent with the laborious human coding efforts of the last six years (i.e. we seek to achieve intercoder reliability between AI and humans .8 or better). We conclude by offering recommendations on next steps for both Cashore’s team and collaborators, as well as those who might want to apply the 4PT framework beyond sustainability policy and governance considerations. Appendices A reviews our approach to coding articles beyond the two distinctions as a way to generate propositions about preliminary explanations for results and Appendix B which provides examples from cases with which to assist in sorting initially “ambiguous” cases. Section 2: ## a) Key Distinctions: Utility and Problem Solving Orientations The specific coding task appears at first glance seems quite straightforward i.e. to distinguish whether scholarly and practitioner policy analyses emphasise, or not, “utility” and “problem solving” orientations (Table 1). | Table1: The Four Problem Types (4PT)
Simplified Distinctions | Utility Dominates? YES | Utility Dominates? No | | ----------------------------------------------------------------- | ---------------------- | --------------------- | | **Problem Solving Dominates? YES** | TYPE 1 | TYPE 4 | | **Problem Solving Dominates? NO** | TYPE 2 | TYPE 3 | These two distinctions originally emerged from Cashore’s reflections, individually and with colleagues, about why so many sustainability policy experts have disagreed for over 35 years about how to think about, and also manage, critical environmental challenges facing the planet highlighted by climate, biodiversity and forest degradation. (Again, while derived from the sustainability policy and governance cases, the 4PT distinctions are meant to be applied to any “on the ground” problems facing policy makers and society including health care, education, child care, education and so on). The origins of the 4PT distinctions followed Cashore’s recognition, having only worked in interdisciplinary forestry, environment and policy schools, that longstanding largely unconscious biases among himself, his colleagues, and experts generally, about how to study the world of sustainability policy and governance, helped explain longstanding debates about not only about how to conceive of, and measure, sustainability challenges, but also recommended courses of policy action for managing the environment, including climate, species extinctions, biodiversity loss, as well as forests and natural resources management. What Cashore noticed was two key divides within these debates. ***The first distinction*** was whether sustainability scholars and practitioners believed that enhancing **“utility”** outcomes was the key task facing sustainability policy analysis. He noted that a large group of scholars generally felt that the more utility was enhanced, the better off society will be. While this causal belief is widely diffused among a range of analysts, it is also hotly debated by scholars and analysts who either oppose utility as a guiding orientation, and/or seek other ways to frame desired outcomes. Hence, Cashore recognized that the belief that utility enhancement was the preferable, from those that did not, was an excellent way to distinguish sustainability policy and governance analyses. ***Cashore’s second distinction*** came from his recognition that while some narrowed their conclusion to assessing how to solve or effectively manage the clearly specified problem at hand- such as such as saving a particular species from extinction, avoiding resource collapse of a specified fish stock, or limiting warming to 1.5 degrees above pre-industrial levels – others brought in analytical frames that allowed for a deliberations over not only how, but ***whether*** the problem at hand could or should be solved. That is, this latter group applied adjudicating frameworks that were developed as a way to address ***any*** particular problem – regardless as to its unique features - that might find its way to the societal or policy agenda in mind. Rather than generating research and analysis to shed light on how to manage a specific policy challenge, this group was united by developing frameworks that allow for consideration of not acting at all, or acting in a way that does not effectively manage the problem at hand. Section 3: ## b) Conceptual Foundations: Utility and Problem Contingent Labels While these distinctions showed great promise, the next task was to elaborate their meanings so that their influence could be better identified and assessed. Section 4: ### The utility label covers a broad range Cashore’s 4PT framework notes that the term “utility” is generally applied and defined broadly. It includes for example, those trained in welfare economics who, largely focusing on income or economic growth, convert others outcomes to “utility equivalents” in order to monetize, and compare, human preferences for green space and nature, with their direct interest in generating income. The term “utility” also incorporates the broader philosophy of “utilitarianism” that seeks to advance outcomes that produce the “greatest good for the greatest number in the long run”. It is also used more generally as a term to discuss whether, in terms of enhancing individual or collection satisfactions, there is any “utility” in a proposed course of action? For Cashore, the rationale for use of “utility” as a label was that it best captures the general belief behind a range of analyses that their goal is to improve “**human satisfactions”.** This is an important clarification because the utility as a term has a decidedly anthropogenetic orientation in which policy is geared towards improving the interests of humans either today or in the future. This, in turn, is shaped by some kind of homage and widespread work developing theories relevant to unpacking human **“self-interest”** .What is important for coding on the 4PT framework is that the concern itself is what is important – i.e. **the utility label in the 4PT framework is shorthand to capture those who are motivated to advance human self-interested satisfactions that incorporate a range of more specific satisfaction seeking rationales** This orientation in turn, allows coders to identify efforts to promote and understand **self-interested behaviours at multiple levels** of sustainability policy and governance including individual, organizational, nation state decisions as well as those within international governance arenas and global governance agreements. Hence, the utility label includes, for coding purposes, analyses focused on aggregating **individual utility** , **organizational utility** (such as companies returning shareholder value), or even **states’ utility** by maximizing their own self-interested preferences. This category incorporates, rather than distinguishes, longstanding debates among neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars within international relations about whether self-interested, utility chasing states, are motivated to achieve relative or absolute gains. (The 4PT framework notes that debates about relative or absolute gains have undermine the similar quest to advance utility seeking interests, including, at the state level, relying on some conception of “national interest” that improves the satisfaction of its population through some kind of beneficial “rational” outcome. **Hence, this broad use of the utility label focuses coders on identifying any kind of analysis that is motivated by enhancing human satisfaction through self-interested preference maximization** . This allows coders to capture in one category a range of scholarship and analysis that although diverse and nuanced, are united by their underlying orientation in emphasizing individual, organizational and state based preferences that pay homage to improving human “satisfactions” at multiple levels. Section 5: ### The problem solving label: distinguishing analyses that target “how”, not “whether” --- The term “problem solving” is also used broadly to capture a range of expertise and approaches that undertake research with which to solve or effectively manage a particular “on the ground” problem. That is, they eschew asking “whether” a problem can be effectively managed, and instead ask, given the problem at hand, how can it be effectively managed? This category then incorporates a broad group of scholars and analysts who are united in their quest to understand and effectively management an “on the ground” problem such as the extinction of a particular species or limiting warming to no more than 1.5/2 °C above pre-industrial levels promoted through the Paris Accord. This means that the analyses they bring, and conclusions they make, will go back to implications for generating knowledge useful for problem solving of a problem, or a class of problems. Similarly, these analysts will never apply universal pre-established theories to assess whether the problem at hand ***ought*** to be solved. This is not their interest. Likewise while their research may carry lessons for other problems, they do not seek to generate universal theories that can be applied to any kind of problem. Put another way, this group of scholars would, for these reasons, avoid any quest for developing a theoretical orientation, or set of recommendations, that went beyond the problem or class of problems from which their research was derived. The practical result is that many in this category tend to carefully assess problem structure or “key features of the problem” they are attempting to shed light on how to better manage or solve. This is important. The reason they eschew any effort to develop a universal way to address any problem is because, by disconnecting from problem structure, the approach applied elsewhere might not effectively manage or solve another class of problems – i.e. it might even make those worse off. This means that they are largely conducting research in which its lessons are “problem contingent”. Any effort to generate lessons beyond a class of problems that is offered as a way to think about, and manage, all problems, is evidence that a scholarly analysis is not in this category. More specifically, any effort that concludes it is not “feasible” or “rational” to address a particular problem at hand that draws on a pre-established theory or framework that that was developed to analyse any and all problem is outside of this sorting category. This elaboration leads to the identification of a more nuanced table of which human and AI coders must be aware. Coders will note that I have also changed the Y axis label to “problem contingent” which I treat as synonymous as “problem solving” dominates, but also more useful for coding purposes. I keep the broader term in the first table as it is more accessible to the uninitiated coder, while bringing in the more specific term once they have read these first few overview pages. | Table 2: The Four Problem Types
Expanded Distinctions | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? YES | Utility dominates: Does the analysis, conclusions, and theories treat individuals, organizations and states as largely self-interested, satisfaction driven entities that seek some kind “utility enhancing” outcome? NO | | ----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? YES** | TYPE 1 Utility / Contingent | TYPE 4 Not Utility / Contingent | | **Problem contingent: Is the analysis, conclusions, and theories generated from, and narrowed to, understanding, improving, or solving, a clearly specified “on the ground” problem or class of problems? NO** | TYPE 2 Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | TYPE 3 Not Utility / Not Problem Contingent (Universal) | In sum, at the risk of repetition, let me be clear: these frameworks offer ways to deliberate over ***not only how, but whether, a specific problem can be effectively managed given a broader set of considerations beyond the problem*** itself. Section 6: ## C) Examples of Not Problem Contingent (Universal Approaches) As I discuss below I refer to the major strand of Optimization school of sustainability thought and practice known as “cost-benefit analysis” (CBA) as a leading example of this approach. This is because CBA frameworks assesses whether or not a policy intervention governing a particular problem will be able to produce, or not, aggregate utility benefits. Under this approach, if policy designers cannot find an intervention to address a problem at hand that will enhance overall utility (i.e. possible policy interventions are all deemed to undermine aggregate utility) then the “rational” answer from this CBA approach is not to act. There are, to be sure, many other strands of the Optimization school. For example, within international relations, both neo-realist and neo-liberal international relations scholars seek to generate theories of state preference, including longstanding debates about whether states maximize their preferences owing to absolute or relative gains. What is important for the 4PT framework is not their debates about underlying motivations that shape a given state’s “self-interest” preferences, but that both neo-realist and neo-liberal institutionalist scholars develop their theories and frameworks to explain ***state behaviour in general*** – i.e. they seek universal understandings of utility chasing state preference that explain, but are not derived from, their choices about how to manage a specified problem. That is, they develop and apply theories of state preferences that apply beyond a particular problem, but are meant to explain state behaviour over a particular problem. To repeat, their project is to develop universal theories of state action that are not derived from, or limited to, any specific problem or issue states happen to be deliberating over. These theories of state action also inform recommendations from these scholar about how states might act go address a specific problem in order to better achieve their pre-existing self-interested preferences (i.e. how to “optimize” outcomes given existing understandings of state preferences). This is why, as I discuss below, so many IR scholars make the case for addressing the global climate crisis based on the argument that doing so will advance state preferences, rather than asking whether a theory of state preferences is the most useful way to think about, and manage, particular kinds of problems. This same dilemma facing cost-benefit scholars who so believe in the utility enhancing motivations that they constantly argue, and work so hard to demonstrate – even despite overwhelming evidence to the contrary – that an emphasis on the utility undermining effects of the climate crisis is the most useful way to conceive of, and develop policy options for, averting global warming. As I discuss below, strands within the “Compromise School” of sustainability thought and practice are also replete with a range of efforts to develop universal theories of stakeholder engagement, legitimacy formation, and conflict resolution that, while rejecting utility chasing theories, nonetheless also develop frameworks for understanding motivations of individuals, organizations and states, that are also developed to understand, and prescribe solutions for, a range of problems that may come across the desk of policy officials. For example, there is one strand of the Compromise school known as “alternative dispute resolution” ADR that offers a way for stakeholder to agree on litigation avoiding natural resources management agreements that draws on universal theories of individual and organization behaviour as a way foster a compromise agreement among disparate interests. While drawing on theories that extend far beyond, and reject, utility motivations as far to narrow an approach to human behaviour, they share the same mission in developing university theories as a way to generate an understanding about not only how, but whether, the “on the ground” issue, or set of issues, can be resolved given the overall focus on conflict resolution rather than solving and effectively managing, the “on the ground” problem that spark the ADR in the first place. Another stand of the compromise school has been nurtured by those studying philosophy which seeks to generate a set of norms and protocols for making difficult decisions – such as whether to save someone stopped on the train tracks that might risk the lives of many on the train. In these cases, pre-existing approaches for developing normative theory based protocols for doing so – not based on utility per se but on claims to higher level “ethics” are turned to as a way to have society and policy makers thinks about managing difficult questions – from species extinctions that results from enhancing economic growth – to saving lives during a pandemic when the isolationist policies for doing say may undermine children’s cognitive development. In these cases what is important for the 4PT framework is that the adjudicating analysis that result are meant to help policy makers and society make decisions to address any problem that might come along, and are not derived, narrowed to, or contingent, on a particular class of problems. Section 7: ## d) Examples of Problem Contingent Approaches (Not Universal) In contrast, and as I elaborate below, members of Commons and Prioritization schools narrowing that adjudicating frameworks to only address, and are indeed derived from, a particular “on the ground” problem or class of problems – whether it be for utility enhancing (Commons) or non-utility (Prioritization) motivations. There are many strands within each of these schools that reinforce “problem contingent” rather than non-problem contingent universal (i.e.. meant to be applied to any problems) frameworks. As I discuss below the most famous utility seeking, problem contingent strand within sustainability studies are those seeking to understand, and effectively manage, “tragedies of the commons” that, if not well managed, produce avoidable resource collapse. Here, scholars seek to understand key features of the problem – such as Ostrom’s fundamental point that some commons tragedies – such as fish that swim - cannot be served by “excluding” access – and hence require different solutions to effectively manage them. The point for the “problem contingent” classification here is that scholars like Ostrom and her students who focus on “non-excludable” tragedies, only develop theories and approaches based on these key features. That is, they do not research, and have little to say, problems that do not share these key features. There are many likeminded strands about developing problem contingent approaches by those who do not share the Common’s utility orientation, but do share is focus on how to effectively manage specified “on the ground” problems – i.e. they eschew considerations about “whether” a specified problem can be solved, to deliberating over how it can solved or effectively managed. For example, Tribe’s work on species extinctions rejected analytical universal approaches offered by extant “policy sciences” framework, to show how any consideration to saving a particular species must begin, and end, based on the knowledge about what kind of conservation efforts is required to maintain the viability of that species. While Tribe and others like him turned to debates in philosophy and the study of “ethics”, he rejected as problematic any effort to address species extinctions that might produce an outcome that caused species extinctions. Hence universal adjudicating frames about particular protocols for assessing policy dilemmas – based on universal theories of “ethics”, was in fact, highly unethical. Others in this line of thinking within international relations have pointed to the “on the ground” problem of slavery. For this group, like species extinctions, the only way to eradicate slavery to develop policies and interventions based on achieving no-slavery. The idea of applying cost-benefitanalysis, or alternative dispute resolution, to assessing not only how, but whether, slavery can be eradicated is anathema to this strands of the prioritization school because it opens the door to consider policy options in which slavery might continue. Hence for these scholars, adjudicating frameworks are always derived from knowledge to manage a specified problems (or class of problems with similar structural features and attributes, such as Ostrom’s non-excludability tragedies). These distinctions have led Cashore to elaborate four categories to include these broader distinctions (Table 2) in which we can now further narrow “problem solving” to “problem contingent” which more accurately labels theories and frameworks and course of action that are never meant to be universal, but only applicable to a class of problems Section 8: ## e) Examples of the Resulting Four Problem Types (4PT) Now that we have covered how the 4PT framework defines utility and problem solving distinctions, and reviewed examples of universal versus problem contingent approaches, we are now in a position to identify examples of approaches classified within each: Type 1 (utility, problem contingent); Type 2 (utility, not problem contingent); Type 3 (not utility, not problem contingent); Type 4 (not utility, problem contingent). This section will, out of necessity, repeat some of the examples above. This is by design: repetition of examples will provide clarity when the same ones are used to distinguish different parts of the classification framework. In addition, this part is important since the coding is focused on the resulting four distinctions – so understanding how the above review figures for making decision about where to place a range of literatures is greatly facilitated by giving these concrete examples. This review, in turn, paves the way for a broader discussion of four key schools of sustainability that reinforce, but are also much broader, than the 4PTs. While broader, inclusion of discussion of the schools is expected to generate knowledge that will facilitate and enhance and generate a nuanced approach to coding the 4PTs. Section 9: ### i. Type 1: Utility Dominates, Problem Contingent **Type 1** then sorts those that are motivated by utility chasing orientations, but narrow their focus to a specified “on the ground” problem that is seen as undermining utility enhancement. Those scholars that are sorted into this category seek to generate analyses and ideas for intervening that improve utility outcomes for that problem. These approaches target the problem or class of problems they seek to ameliorate. Their interest is not in developing universal theories to address any and all sustainability problems. This means that they generate recommendations and analyses based on their problem or class of problems in isolation from other problems that might have different features. And they certainly would never apply a theoretical or analytical framework to discuss whether the problem should be solved. There are countless examples of this kind of orientation. For example, I have a colleague who studies the issue of taxis idling looking for riders in downtown Singapore when it appears that they would make more income (utility) by driving to the airport. This problem then can be solved by reducing “information asymmetry” though some kind of intervention – such as advertising or sharing this knowledge, or through some kind of regulation reducing an ability to idle. In this case, interventions are adjudicated only for their ability to manage that particular problem - regardless as to the possible effects of the “effective policy intervention” in reducing overall aggregate utility – such as those case in which limited government resources are better placed on managing another problem that would lead to higher utility. As I discussed above and elaborate below Type 1 sorting would also include a large group of scholars and practitioners working on “collective action theory” in general, and its application to sustainability through the “tragedy of the commons metaphor” in particular. This is an important distinction. Collective Action theorists are motivate by eliminating utility undermining outcomes such as rubber necking on highways, hording of toilet paper during pandemics, or over harvesting that leads to collapse of fish stocks or water supplies. These approaches, which draw on the classic “prisoners dilemma” in which, in the absence of an ability to develop and comply with, a collective agreements, two prisoners in different cells both have a “self-interest” to confess because if they do not, but their partner in crime does, they will be handed a much higher and longer prison sentence. This “confessing/confessing” strategy is considered to the most effective utility enhancing approach given no collective agreement (an outcome known as the “Nash equilibrium) but it is not the most utility enhancing solution. The most utility enhancing solution is for both not to confess, as this will lead to lower prison sentences overall. In this case, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions to allow for both not to confess. In the sustainability world, the issue then is how to build and design collective action institutions such that harvesting of the resource in question is less than or equal to the biological rate or reproduction. In all these cases, analyses are focused on human satisfactions, but only around the specific problem in question. As I discussed above and in more detail below, Elinor Ostrom’s path to earning the Nobel prize in economics was sparked by her recognition that for some utility undermining tragedies, highlighted by fish, excludability was not possible. This mean that traditional interventions to solve this utility undermining commons tragedies that turned to private property or state ownership, and that assumed excludability was possible, were ill suited to addressing those utility undermining resource depletion tragedies. Ostrom then turned to assessing how to effectively manage these “common pool resources” (CPRs) which she posited were better addressed through local resource governing innovations. The point for coders here is that all of these efforts paid attention to the key features of the problem at hand – specifically those contextual and contingent factors that explained the particular utility undermining outcome for the problem at hand. Collective action theorists in general, and commons tragedy scholars in particular, only generalize based on the specific problem at hand, or a class of problems such as those commons tragedies in which excludability is not possible. They would never therefore apply their lessons to managing all problems nor bringing in an assessment as to whether the problem can be solved. They only look at the how to do so, not whether. Section 10: ### ii. Type 2: Utility Dominates, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) In contrast, **Type 2** literatures share an interest in understanding and championing utility seeking preferences, but they reject a focus that only includes attention to the specific problem at hand. The logic behind this, whether CBA that targets and aggregates individual preferences, or IR theories that target and aggregate state preferences, is that that group believes that it is unwise to assess policy options for ameliorating a specified problem without considering, and finding a way to incorporate, the result of this decision on other kinds of problems. Their logic is clear: if solving or effectively managing one specific kind of utility undermining activity – such as say, over harvesting timber – results in a set of policy and governance decisions that might preclude more lucrative activities – such as say the introduction of copper mining and its promise of increase wages – then policy officials have undermined their responsibilities in managing for the greatest amount of utility with which to benefit the welfare of citizens. This category also captures the IR scholars above who also seek to generalize about the processes through which states do, or should, maximizing their preferences – often in the name of the “national interest”- that are then applied to assess how – and sometimes even whether – the problem at hand can be effectively managed given state preferences. Hence those analysts that turn to state preferences as a way to think about how to manage and develop policy response – whether it be, as discussed below, Nordhaus’ reasoning that optimal state responses to the utility undermining impacts of the climate crisis will produce a 3.2 degree world – in contrast to the 1.5 outcome a problem contingent approach of the IPCC. It also applied, as discussed below, to neo-liberal institutionalist Keohane whose climate research turns to his theory of state preferences for policy solutions, rather than interrogating the contingent nature of the climate crisis itself. Hence whether CBA, or conflict resolution or a host of other likeminded approaches, Type 4 classifies those analytical approaches that are not offered as contingent on solving a particular problem but as a way to adjudicate the problem at hand by apply a universal way to think about, measure, and identify, and project policy interventions. Hence, these approach there expands consideration, and offering a framework for doing so, about whether the problem at hand **can, or should,** be effectively managed based on some kind of approach to preference formation. Section 11: ### iii. Type 3: Utility Does Not Dominate, Not Problem Contingent (Universal) There are many diverse approaches that are nonetheless united by their **Type 3 status** : i.e. analytical frames for understanding, explaining and prescribing policy actions that generate protocols for doing so that eschew utility motivations as a dominant force or outcome, and instead reflect on a diverse set of motivations with which to understand, and prescribe courses of action. These approaches are united by rejecting the treatment of non-utility outcomes as “irrational” and instead wonder how to understand and incorporate cultural values, social practices, environmental norms, that produce some kind of way to both understand the world and also provide the basis for recommendations about how to include a diverse set of ideas, organizations that generate legitimate process and wide accepted outcomes. As reviewed above when discussing universal approaches, while they reject utility as a dominant motivation, they share the idea that pre-existing theories and frameworks can be applied to adjudicate not only how, but whether, a particular problem can be solved. In other words, with these approaches – whether originating from a particular set of ethical frames in philosophy, sociology, geography or critical theory, one never knows for sure whether applying a particular one of these approaches to a particular problem will result in the problem at hand being solved or effectively managed. In other words, in contrast to the teleological orientation of Types 1 and 4, Type 3 approaches work from a preexisting framework – or develop a framework from a case that is meant to apply to any chase – rather than developing a framework that only pertains to that problem or class of problems. Section 12: ### iv. Type 4: Utility Does Not Dominate, Problem Contingent For these reasons **Type 4** frameworks are distinguished, like Type 1, on their ability to help understand, and ameliorate, a specified problem from the Paris Accord’s 1.5/2 degree objectives, to a particular species being threatened with extinction. However unlike Type 1 reinforcing approaches, Type 4 problems not only reject utility as the guiding force, they usually find that when those chasing utility outcomes are effective, they usually cause, or exacerbate, a range of Type 4 problems. The classic example comes to us from the identification of two distinct problems that are outside of the sustainability challenges to which Cashore devoted his empirical work. The first problem is that of slavery – i.e. the act of one human having property rights over another human being – i.e. “owning” them. A range of scholarly analyses assessing this problem have found that the only way to ensure slavery is dramatically reduced or eradicated is to begin, and end, with the required outcome: i.e. no one, for any reason, is allowed to own another person. Many apply relevant frameworks have not only found that (Type 1 and 2) utility chasing motivations was behind slavery in the first place, but that application of Type 3, 2 frames permits deliberations over whether slavery ought to be considered – which, by definition, undermines an ability to achieve the desired outcome. The second example is anti-colonial norms in which reducing or averting an attempt by one country to take over another can only be achieved by teleological approaches that beginning, and end, with the absence of colonialism. Scholarly analysis reinforcing Type 4 hence would reject neo-liberal and neo-realist IR theories as permitting an understanding of state preferences that could also consider colonial type behaviours, rather than assessing mechanisms for ensuring these possibilities are never considered. As I discuss below, a number of analytical frames within environmental studies are consistent with Type 4 reasoning. Section 13: ## f) Coding Challenges The above review will most certainly facilitate, and place in context, efforts to code qualitative documents against the 4PTs. This facilitates, rather than overcomes, several challenges that a code book must incorporate. First, while some documents may seem straightforward, others may provide a nuanced or even contradictory account that makes sorting more challenging. The solution to this is to provide, and justify, systematic protocols to facilitate sorting of what we refer to as “ambiguous” cases below. Second, different types of documents will need slightly different ways to disentangle the 4PTs. For example, our careful review of journal articles on sustainability policy and governance requires laborious coding of highly complex articles. Our original focused on the Journal of Regulation and Governance was used to both revise our 4PT distinctions, and then to go back, recode, and assess intercoder reliability. However, our approach to coding abstracts for course syllabi at Yale’s environment schools was much swifter and faster – as we only needed to see whether the course championed a utility outcome or not, and whether it was focused on applying its approach to specific on the ground problems. Both coding exercises permitted the development of dominant “key words” within each both deductively and inductively. This means that one solution for accelerating coding through quantitively analysis of text is to identify the prevalence of different key words associated with one problem type over the others. Of course this approach is not perfect because sometimes some concepts such as “cost effectiveness” analysis can be used to achieve any kind of Type 4, 3 2 and 1 outcomes, while “cost benefit” reinforces Type 2. It may be difficult for using a key word approach alone to therefore uncover these nuances. Hence, while this approach may not be as nuanced as the human coding orientation, it promises to provide more swift data that itself, will be useful. And as along as standardizes approach is taken, it will still produce useful data even if that set might be slightly different than if laborious human coders had applied our more nuanced approach. Finally, and to integrate these different efforts, our ultimate goal is to draw on AI techniques that might best replicate human coding, but in much less time. Another challenges concerns whether an article is simply categorized by asking and answer the two distinguishing questions, or, to save, time, whether a sorting approach can be undertaken through a “branching tree” metaphore: i.e. first ask: “utility” if so, (1 or 2) then ask “problem contingent?, if so 1 or 4. Under this approach Type 3 emerges because other types are excluded, rather than affirmatively coding for Type 3. The problem with this approach – and which tripped us up initially – was that Type 3 was more likely to include ambiguous cases and or cases beyond our universe (i.e. they did not actually pertain to policy and governance). Irja Vormedal and Lars H. Gulbrandsen Fridtjof Nansen Institute, Lysaker, Norway Abstract Research recognizes that strategic business interests can provide an important driver of private regulation, even in the absence of signiﬁcant societal pressure and non-governmental organization-constructed demand. This article examines a range of com- petition and collective action-related interests that can motivate ﬁrms to promote and adopt certiﬁcation schemes. We pay particular attention to the hitherto underexplored collective action interest to safeguard common-pool resources, upon which an industry may depend to sustain yields. Based on a case study of salmon aquaculture certiﬁcation, the article argues that while the corporate motives repertoire includes strengthening competitiveness and industry reputation, safeguarding shared waters for culturing salmon is key to explaining industry commitment to and adoption of private regulation in this sector. Keywords: certiﬁcation, common-pool resource, private authority, private regulation, salmon aquaculture. 1. Introduction In 2007, a severe crisis hit the booming salmon farming industry in Chile. It was caused by elevated levels of sea lice leading to a major outbreak of infectious salmon anemia (ISA). Salmon farmers were forced to fallow considerably, which resulted in substantial downsizing and ﬁnancial write-downs in the years to come. This crisis was an example of the “tragedy of the commons” – a theory of a situation within a shared resource sys- tem where individual users acting rationally and independently according to their own self-interest behave contrary to the common good, leading to resource depletion (Hardin 1968). In the Chilean case, the rapid growth of aquaculture led to uncontrolled contamination of shared waters used for salmon farming by para- sites, virus, and bacteria, and combined with a lack of public regulation and industry preparedness, allowed ISA to spread rapidly between facilities. In retrospect, the crisis seems to have prompted leading ﬁrms to begin pushing for private regulation of the industry, notably through the broad adoption of the Aquaculture Stewardship Council (ASC) – a non-state certiﬁcation scheme that sets standards and rules for sustainable aquaculture management. Indeed, private regulation – deﬁned as the standards and rules set by non-state actors to regulate the behavior of corporations – has become a prominent mode of addressing governance challenges in a globalized world econ- omy (Mayer & Gerefﬁ2010; Auld & Gulbrandsen 2013). The proliferation of private regulation is widely under- stood as a response to economic globalization, and the inability or unwillingness of governments to ameliorate pressing social and environmental problems related to transnational production, distribution, and consumption (Auld & Gulbrandsen 2013). Frustrated with the lack of government intervention, global civil society has increas- ingly targeted corporations directly, using public naming-and-shaming and boycott campaigns to persuade and coerce global businesses to adopt private regulation (Bendell 2004; Vogel 2005, 2010). Certiﬁcation and eco- labeling schemes – established across sectors such as forestry, agriculture, and ﬁsheries – arguably represent the most rigorous effort insofar as they involve mandatory third party veriﬁcation of compliance with performance- based standards (Gulbrandsen 2014). When certiﬁcation requires companies to implement stricter standards than prescribed by government regulations, it is also expected to raise business costs considerably. Correspondence: Lars H. We investigate the relative explanatory power of business competition and collective action motives in an in-depth study of the global and burgeoning salmon aquaculture industry – the industrialized culturing of salmon in underwater farms. Salmon aquaculture has become a large global business, representing 60 percent of the world’s salmon consumption and 70 percent of global trade in farmed seafood. Its rapid industrialization has had substantial environmental repercussions (Bunting 2013), and an associated demand for private regulation (Bush et al. 2013; Vormedal 2017), accelerating the uptake of the ASC salmon standard. In our empirical investi- gation, however, we observe that key producers commit to and adopt ASC certiﬁcation even when there is little or no demand-side pressure for certiﬁcation and no price premium to be gained. The puzzle we examine is the following: Why do aquaculture companies commit to and adopt relatively costly ASC certiﬁcation when there is little demand for certiﬁed salmon and no price premium to be harvested? Our study focuses mainly on salmon certiﬁcation in Norway – the world’s largest production region accounting for 54 percent of global supplies – home to the world’s leading multinational corporations, with major operations in Chile and other countries. Several strategic business motives have been identiﬁed in the literature, such as a desire to strengthen an industry’s reputation, widen product differentiation, harvest price premiums, and harmonize an existing sprawl of private standards. To provide analytical clarity, we categorize relevant strategic motives as anchored in either competition or collective action interests. While competition interests refer to the adoption and promotion of cer- tiﬁcation to gain competitive advantages through differentiation, thereby increasing proﬁts, market shares, and sales volumes (e.g. Porter 1985; Delmas et al. 2004), collective action interests refer to the adoption and promo- tion of certiﬁcation to address common challenges, such as industry reputation (e.g. Klein 1997; Bartley 2007), and the unsustainable management of a common-pool resource upon which an industry depends to sustain yields (Ostrom 1990, 2005). Analytically, we pay particular attention to a distinct collective action motive that remains underexplored in the literature on private authority: strategic business interest in safeguarding common-pool resources. While strengthening competitiveness and boosting the industry’s reputation also encouraged businesses to implement the ASC standard, we emphasize the centrality of a corporate motive to protect shared waters used for salmon production from biological contamination by parasites, viruses, and bacteria through the implementation of certiﬁcation. 2. Business motives for adopting certiﬁcation After decades of debate on the role of non-state actors in global politics, it is now widely recognized that states share rulemaking authority with non-state actors across economic sectors (e.g. Rosenau & Czempiel 1992; Risse- Kappen 1995; Cutler et al. 1999; Hauﬂer 2001; Josselin & Wallace 2001; Hall & Biersteker 2002; Vogel 2010; Gulbrandsen 2014). Since the 1990s, non-state governance institutions that regulate global corporations and transnational economic activities have proliferated to compensate for the lack of government regulation or to serve functions that have traditionally been the task of governments (e.g. Falkner 2003; Gulbrandsen 2010; Büthe & Mattli 2011; Auld & Gulbrandsen 2013; Green 2014). Transnational sustainability certiﬁcation schemes arguably represent the most advanced case of non-state governance efforts, comparable to public regulation in their prescription of mandatory and often costly behavioral changes, and the formal penalization of non- compliance (Cashore 2002; Auld et al. 2008, 2015). The role of social movements, transnational advocacy networks, and NGOs as initiators of certiﬁcation schemes and mobilizers of companies is widely documented. In the era of globalization – as markets and produc- tion networks have surpassed national governmental systems – transnational NGOs are increasingly targeting producers and buyers along their value chains (Overdevest 2004; Mayer & Gerefﬁ2010) to coerce and entice companies to engage in standard-setting processes for establishing certiﬁcation schemes (Boström 2006; Bos- tröm & Hallström 2010). In so doing, they have used a combination of “sticks” – such as naming-and-shaming campaigns targeting big brands, public protests, and boycotts – and “carrots” – such as promises of improved industry or ﬁrm reputation and access to eco-conscious markets (Keck & Sikkink 1998; Wapner 2002; Bendell 2004; Cashore et al. 2004; Gulbrandsen 2006, 2010; Stolle & Micheletti 2013). The focal targets of NGO © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 2 I. Vormedal and L. H. Gulbrandsen Salmon aquaculture certiﬁcation Page 3: campaigns were retailers and well-known brands near the distribution end of global supply chains (Bartley et al. 2015). A common business response has been to put pressure on producers to adopt sustainability certiﬁca- tion schemes that could improve social and environmental conditions at the site of production (Gulbrandsen 2010; Bartley et al. 2015). Many of the reasons why companies adopt certiﬁcation schemes are clearly constructed and maintained by NGO power. In the absence of NGO pressure along a producer’s value chain, the extent to which eco-labeled products would harvest price premiums or increase market access is, in many cases, questionable. However, even in the absence of NGO pressure, producers may use certiﬁcation as a tool to promote their own strategic or col- lective industry interests – a motive that is recognized in the literature but warrants closer examination. In the following sections we identify and discuss strategic business motives for adopting certiﬁcation schemes from two complementary perspectives, namely self-interest related to competition and collective action. 2.1. Competition interests and certiﬁcation Competition refers to the rivalry between ﬁrms or sellers in a market to achieve key business objectives, such as increased proﬁts, sales volumes, and market shares (Khemani & Shapiro 1993). Individual ﬁrms are always look- ing for competitive advantages that can give them a more proﬁtable and favorable position within their industry (Porter 1980, 1985). Competitive advantages can be derived from a ﬁrm’s internal resources, such as human or organizational capabilities, or knowledge and technologies – which determine its operational efﬁciency and effectiveness. But they may also be rooted in the ﬁrm’s ability to respond strategically and shape its external environment (Barney 1991; Grant 1991). Low cost and differentiation represent the two main sources of competitive advantage. According to Porter (1985), they give rise to three generic strategies for improving ﬁrm performance, namely: cost leadership, differentiation, and focus. Here we examine differentiation and focus. To gain a competitive advantage through differentiation, a ﬁrm seeks uniqueness and superior performance along a certain dimension valued by existing or potential buyers. By distinguishing themselves from rivals, ﬁrms expect to be rewarded with a price premium, and/or larger sales volumes and market shares. To gain competitive advantage through focus, ﬁrms can target a particular segment of the market in a bid to improve sales within it, either by offering lower than average prices (cost leadership) or by providing a particular uniqueness and superiority valued by this mar- ket segment (differentiation) (see Porter 1980, 1985; Delmas et al. 2004). Drawing on the literature, we argue that business competition can lead ﬁrms to adopt certiﬁcation schemes in at least three key ways. First, ﬁrms that move quickly to seek a particular form of differentiation, or target a spe- ciﬁc, emerging market segment, can expect to reap ﬁrst-mover advantages in that market, that is, the initial bene- ﬁts accruing to those who successfully become the ﬁrst occupants of a market segment. In Porter’s (1985) terminology, validating your “good” environmental and social performance through eco-labeling reﬂects a strate- gic effort to differentiate and focus, to distinguish your products as “superior” according to environmental and social criteria, and to target a growing eco-conscious market segment with these products. In so doing, some ﬁrms seek to reap ﬁrst-mover advantages by moving quickly to increase their eco-labeled supply, swooping up paramount customers and landing key contracts, giving them a proﬁtable market share of the eco-segment. As previous research demonstrates, certiﬁcation can widen market access and provide price premiums for eco- labeled products, depending on market and product characteristics (see e.g. Auld et al. 2008). Sustainability certi- ﬁcation may therefore be seen as a demand-driven competitive strategy for growing a ﬁrm’s proﬁts and market share (Hoffman 2000; Green 2014). Second, ﬁrst movers who are able to shape rules and standards can tailor the provisions to match their techni- cal and operational requirements, leaving late movers with higher switching costs (Mattli & Buthe 2003). This can give the ﬁrst movers a signiﬁcant advantage over late movers who might face technical and ﬁnancial hurdles that prevent them from upgrading operations and meeting market requirements (Rotherham 2003). Third, businesses may subscribe to certiﬁcation to build an individual, ﬁrm-speciﬁc reputation or brand image (Delmas et al. 2004; Vogel 2005; Auld 2006). Companies can thus create a public reputation as greener or more responsible than the competition, which may even result in greater sales and market shares (Green 2014). © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 3 Salmon aquaculture certiﬁcation I. Vormedal and L. H. Gulbrandsen Page 4: In sum, certiﬁcation can give companies at least three competitive advantages: product differentiation, ﬁrst- mover advantages, and improved reputation. We examine each of these motives in our investigation of commit- ment to and uptake of ASC certiﬁcation. 2.2. Collective action interests and certiﬁcation The need to resolve common challenges and collective action problems in an industry can serve as an important motivation behind companies’ support for regulation through certiﬁcation. Drawing on Ostrom (2005), we distin- guish two types of collective goods that can prompt companies to commit to and adopt certiﬁcation schemes. Ostrom identiﬁed two basic attributes of different types of goods: difﬁculty of excluding potential beneﬁciaries and subtractability of use. When those two attributes are dichotomized, as shown in Table 1, they can be used as the deﬁning attributes of four basic types of goods: club goods (referred to as toll goods by Ostrom), private goods, public goods, and common-pool resources (Ostrom 2005, p. 23). Here we examine the two goods that are neither purely public (non-subtractive and non-excludable) nor purely private (subtractive and excludable): club goods and common-pool resources. First, a group of companies may seek to protect and improve their reputation by adopting a certiﬁcation pro- gram (cf. Klein 1997; Spar 1998; Bartley 2007). Drawing on club theory (Buchanan 1965), Prakash and Potoski (2006) argue that certiﬁcation programs produce non-subtractive (non-rival) but potentially excludable beneﬁts to their members, such as a positive environmental reputation (see Table 1). According to this approach, certiﬁca- tion programs can be conceptualized as clubs that induce members to voluntarily improve environmental perfor- mance and exclude free riding (Prakash & Potoski 2006; Potoski & Prakash 2009). Clubs of environmental and social frontrunners that are eager and able to verify claims about their above-average performance may adopt cer- tiﬁcation programs to boost their reputation and distinguish themselves from “irresponsible” free-riders (Prakash & Potoski 2006; Potoski & Prakash 2009). Thus, a group of ﬁrms can be motivated to promote, join, and implement certiﬁcation schemes to solve a collective reputational problem. Second, a certiﬁcation program can potentially serve as a tool to manage common-pool resources (see Table 1). Common-pool resources are typically deﬁned as non-excludable, as it is difﬁcult to exclude anyone from the enjoyment of its beneﬁts and services. They are also typically subtractive, in that the use of a resource by one actor is likely to subtract units of that resource from a ﬁnite amount available for harvesting (Ostrom 2005, pp. 23–24). By contrast, public goods yield non-subtractive beneﬁts that can be enjoyed jointly by many actors. In the absence of private property or adequate regulatory institutions, common-pool resource degradation may result in a “tragedy of the commons” situation in which the natural resource base that enables sustained harvests becomes depreciated to the point of threatening industry survival (cf. Hardin 1968). If an industry depends on a common-pool resource to sustain production yields in the long-term – and gov- ernment regulation appears inadequate to protect this resource from degradation – ﬁrms may turn to certiﬁcation as a form of collective regulatory action that can safeguard the resource. In principle, a responsibly managed, common-pool resource can represent a collective good for an industry, to the extent that it enables producers to sustain yields. The lack of adequate regulatory control, on the other hand, can become a collective action problem (see Olson 1965) insofar as it raises production risks and threatens the proﬁtability of harvests. While the risk of free-riding and a tragedy of the commons situation typically calls for privatization or gov- ernment regulation, Ostrom (1990, 2005) demonstrated how self-organized systems for managing common-pool resources can serve the same purpose. Similarly, we argue, certiﬁcation schemes may function as a private gover- nance tool for dealing with common-pool resource degradation that negatively affects an industry’s operations. Thus, ﬁrms may have an economic self-interest to adopt industry-wide regulation of a common-pool resource Table 1 Four basic types of goods Subtractability of use Low High Difﬁculty of excluding potential beneﬁciaries Low Club goods Private goods High Public goods Common-pool resources Source: Adapted from Ostrom (2005). © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 4 I. Although the motive to safeguard a common-pool resource has been well established in the extensive literature on resource management institutions, it remains under-investigated and poorly understood in the context of non- state certiﬁcation. In sum, we argue that collective action-based motives, in particular to protect or improve the reputation of a group of companies (or an entire industry), and/or to safeguard a common-pool resource upon which an indus- try may depend, represent two potentially important drivers behind company uptake of certiﬁcation schemes. We examine both motives in our empirical investigation of aquaculture certiﬁcation but pay particular attention to the motive to manage common-pool resources through certiﬁcation. 2.3. Method and material The ﬁndings and analyses in this article draw on multiple sources of data. Primarily, we rely on a set of in-depth, semi-structured interviews with several informants in each of the salmon farming companies that has adopted ASC certiﬁcation. Company informants include chief executive ofﬁcers (CEOs); heads of sales; and directors of operation, quality, and environmental management. We also interviewed companies we considered eligible – but had yet to adopt the ASC – to gain an understanding of the certiﬁcation acquisition threshold, and reasons for remaining uncertiﬁed. In total, we interviewed 15 informants at ASC-certiﬁed companies, and 4 informants at non-ASC companies. Upon their request, and to enable full information disclosure, we granted anonymity to most of our industry interviewees. We also interviewed three top-level bureaucrats at the Norwegian ﬁsheries ministry, and a key representative at the World Wildlife Fund (WWF), central to the development of the ASC standard. To arrive at our conclusions about underlying company motives and interests, we asked our interviewees to explain why and how they decided to get certiﬁed, and account for what they thought were the main drivers of this larger trend. We then asked informants to comment and discuss their company’s interests in relation to both competition and collective action motives, to classify and rank interests, and to provide examples of why and how they were relevant and important. We scrutinized the validity of their answers by identifying indicators. For example, an indicator of collective action interests would be efforts to encourage competitors and the industry at large to certify facilities, whereas stated attempts to be a ﬁrst mover and stand out through ASC certiﬁcation indi- cated the presence of competition interests. Our interviews were taped and transcribed. When analyzed, we used pattern matching to compare our expectations with the respondents’ comments and opinions, and to categorize interests across interviews. This process enabled us to identify all motivations, rank their importance, and provide thick descriptions of how they came into play. We used industry reports, sale statistics, export market data, ASC certiﬁcation data, third-party certiﬁcation reports, and other sources to triangulate the interview data. We also drew on secondary literature on the aquaculture industry as well as on certiﬁcation experiences gleaned in other relevant sectors (such as ﬁsheries and forestry) to provide context and relate our ﬁndings to broader patterns and trends. 3. Salmon aquaculture and sustainability certiﬁcation 3.1. Salmon aquaculture problems and the emergence of private regulation Aquaculture is an ancient tradition dating back to carp breeding in 2500 BC China (U.S. Food and Drug Admin- istration [FDA] 2013). During the Medieval and Renaissance eras, ﬁsh farming was practiced on a modest scale throughout Europe and the Americas (Bunting 2013), but in the 19th and 20th centuries, as wild capture ﬁsheries underwent rapid industrialization – aquaculture became a supplementary activity accounting for a miniscule per- cent of global supply (Smith 2012). It was not until commercial overﬁshing began to raise concerns over a deple- tion of wild ﬁsh stocks during the 1970s that aquaculture re-emerged as a viable option for growing the world’s ﬁsh supply. Since 2014, it accounts for over 50 percent of the world’s ﬁsh that is used for food, and future growth of the ﬁsheries sector is likely to come mainly from aquaculture (FAO 2016: 22). Among all cultured species, Atlantic salmon represents the largest and most highly valued of global, farmed- ﬁsh markets (Asche 2012). Methods of breeding salmon in open net cages were developed by local farmers on the Norwegian coast in the 1960s, and spread to other production regions, such as Scotland, Ireland, Canada, and © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 5 Salmon aquaculture certiﬁcation I. Vormedal and L. H. Gulbrandsen Page 6: Chile in the 1970s. During the 1990s – fueled by growing market demand and technical innovation in advanced breeding techniques – salmon aquaculture underwent rapid industrialization and consolidation (Naylor et al. 2003; Asche & Khatun 2006; Olesen et al. 2010; Liu et al. 2013; Norwegian Ministry of Fisheries 2013; Kontali Analyse 2016). Today, Norway represents the largest salmon producer with 54 percent of global supplies, and is home to the world’s four largest companies. The growth and industrialization of salmon aquaculture have also had serious environmental repercussions. A major problem has been the increasing biological contamination of shared waters used for farming by sea lice, a parasite that feeds off the salmon’s ﬂesh and skin. Because salmon is cultured in open net cages, with facilities and farms becoming increasingly denser and more closely located, parasites – but also viruses and bacteria – tend to spread easily via water currents. In recent years, the proliferation of sea lice has begun to threaten the well being, and some claim the very existence, of surrounding wild salmon populations (Institute of Marine Research [IMR] 2014). Medicinal treatments and vaccinations have been widely used, but often without signiﬁcant success as the ﬁsh tend to develop resistance (Leonczek 2013; IMR 2014). Furthermore, the industry has also been unable to fully control and prevent farmed salmon from escaping into the wild, which is said to exacerbate the sea lice problem, cause hybridization of the wild salmon gene pool, and result in offspring hybrids deprived of character- istics crucial to survival and adaptation to conditions in the wild (Hindar & Diserud 2007). Increasing biological contamination of public waters following the growth of salmon farming has raised both reputational and economic challenges for the industry. One the one hand, elevated sea lice levels infecting and threatening the health and livelihood of wild salmon populations represents an environmental problem of great public concern. The industry is now facing substantial public criticism for failing to control sea lice levels, and for prioritizing industry growth over risks posed to the health and existence of wild salmon stocks. This environmen- tal issue translates into a reputational challenge for salmon farming companies, which in turn provides a motive for adopting a certiﬁcation program that would improve their public standing and social license to operate. On the other hand, biological contamination can also raise production risks and threaten industry actors’ ability to sustain yields. Elevated levels of sea lice, virus, and bacteria proliferating within and between farms via currents can cause widespread ﬁsh-disease, high mortality rates, and/or force companies to reduce stock or fallow considerably. We conceive of clean water – deﬁned here as water measuring low to adequate levels of parasites, viruses, and bacteria – as a non-excludable natural resource, as it is difﬁcult to exclude any farmer operating within a relatively clean water area from enjoying its beneﬁts, whether or not they contribute to its provision. We also conceive of clean water in a farming area – such as a Norwegian fjord – as subtractive, even if public water used for aquaculture is neither harvestable nor ﬁnite. But the use of water by one industry actor contributes to increasing infection risk, thereby subtracting from its beneﬁts to other farmers. Indeed, the larger the use of the water for farming within an area, the denser the positioning of cages and farms, and the greater biomass of ﬁsh contained within them, the higher the risk of parasite and disease transmission between farms and different com- panies. We therefore deﬁne clean water in a farming area as a common-pool resource upon which the industry depends to sustain yields, or in other words, reduce the risk of stock reduction, mortality, and in the worst case, fallowing. In this way, clean water can provide utility maximizing economic advantages to companies, and there- fore, a motive for supporting industry-wide regulation that forces all farmers to keep sea lice and disease levels low within their facilities, to eliminate free-riding and reduce production risks associated with parasite and dis- ease transmission. However, government regulation in all major production regions, including the European Union (EU), Canada, and Chile, has been criticized for addressing these problems too slowly and inadequately (see e.g. Alvial et al. 2012; Liu et al. 2013; Carter 2015; Carter & Cazals 2015; Vormedal 2017). Norwegian regulation is consid- ered to be the strictest, enforcing an absolute sea lice limit of 0.5 per adult ﬁsh, and 0.1 during the spring to pro- tect wild smolt migration. A few newer “green” farming licenses are also subject to even stricter limits, set at 0.2 and 0.25. The government also requires companies to report and take immediate action to retrieve farmed salmon escapees, but does not enforce an absolute escapee limit. Critics nevertheless argue that the 0.5 sea lice limit and lenient approach to escapees have failed to counter the threats to wild salmon populations (e.g. Fauchald 2016). In response to inadequate government intervention, calls for private regulation had already emerged at the turn of the century. In contrast to NGOs protesting the sector’s very existence – framing ﬁsh farming as © 2018 John Wiley & Sons Australia, Ltd 6 I. Vormedal and L. H. Gulbrandsen Salmon aquaculture certiﬁcation Page 7: something inherently unnatural with slogans like ”Wild Salmon Don’t Do Drugs” and “Farmed and Danger- ous” (Young & Matthews 2010) – actors such as the WWF began to promote certiﬁcation as a private gover- nance solution to the sector’s problems. Inspired by the success of the Marine Stewardship Council (MSC), a certiﬁcation program for wild-capture ﬁsheries certiﬁcation (Gulbrandsen 2009), they organized naming-and- shaming campaigns and consumer boycotts to persuade producers and buyers to commit to selling sustainably farmed ﬁsh. Some retailers and supermarket chains in Europe and North America adopted their own sustain- ability standards that suppliers must comply with (Bush et al. 2013). Whole Foods, for example, requires sup- pliers to address problems like unsustainable feed, chemical use, and predator control (DesMarais 2013).1 In 2004, the WWF instigated the global aquaculture dialogues, which drew over 2,000 participants including farmers, seafood processors, retailers, and foodservice operators, and resulted in the establishment of the ASC in 2009. The ASC – an independent, non-proﬁt certiﬁcation organization – aspires to become the world’s lead- ing eco-labeling scheme for sustainably farmed seafood. The ASC salmon standard, which became operational in 2013, sets out seven overarching principles for the environmental and social management of farming sites, each of which contains a range of speciﬁc criteria and requirements.2 In many respects, they are much stricter than the standards set by the Norwegian government.3 Both Norway and the ASC regulate the use and release of chemicals, nutrients, and medicines to protect local biodiversity, seabed and water quality, and set formal sea lice limits. Yet the ASC goes further in setting a maxi- mum level of 300 escapees, which effectively prevents actors with signiﬁcant escapee rates from becoming certi- ﬁed, and requires all farms to implement a stricter sea lice limit of 0.1 and engage in monitoring and data collection on the health of the surrounding wild salmon population. Moreover, the ASC set maximum levels for ﬁsh disease and medicinal treatments; marine ingredients in salmon feeds require certiﬁcation of marine and soya-derived ingredients; and smolt production in open net cages is prohibited – all of which are focus areas covered to varying degrees by Norwegian regulations. More generally, to attain ASC certiﬁcation of a salmon farming site, member companies must verify compli- ance with ASC standards by engaging ASC-licensed, third party certiﬁcation organizations. Once a farm is certi- ﬁed, ﬁsh produced at the site is eligible to use the ASC eco-label on its packaging, providing access to markets and retailers with certiﬁcation requirements, and to communicate directly to consumers that their products are sustainably produced. Gaining ASC certiﬁcation may therefore require behavioral and management changes that are more costly and difﬁcult to implement than simply complying with the Norwegian government’s regulations. 3.2. Globalization, exports and Aquaculture Stewardship Council (ASC) certiﬁcation Market demand for certiﬁed and eco-labeled seafood originates in major salmon-consuming countries, such as the United States (US), United Kingdom (UK), France, Germany, and Sweden (Nøstvold et al. 2010; Honkanen & Nilssen 2013).4 Thus far, the demand for eco-labeled salmon is mostly in Europe and North America, but there are differences between markets in terms of focus on and deﬁnition of sustainability. For example, UK and Ger- man retailers appear to be the most rigorous in requiring suppliers to adhere to environmental standards for ﬁsh sourcing and production, while French buyers are perceived to be less rigid and equally focused on social stan- dards, such as workers’ conditions and the protection of local communities (Nøstvold et al. 2010; Honkanen & Nilssen 2013). The market for farmed salmon is global, and Norwegian production is predominantly for export. In 2015, nearly 97 percent of the Norwegian harvest was exported (see Fig. 1). To date, there has been little demand for certiﬁed salmon in Norway, and all of the available ASC-labeled salmon is sold abroad.
